insights: w-nhsh hyh `rwm mkl khyt hshdh (והנחש היה ערום מכל חית השדה) — 'Now the serpent was more subtil' — The word 'arum (ערום) meaning 'subtil' shares a root with 'arom (ערום) meaning 'naked', which appears in 2:25 describing Adam and Eve's innocence; the serpent's cunning plays on the same root that will describe the couple's exposure after eating the fruit.

"mipri `etz hagan" (מפרי עץ הגן) — from the fruit of the tree of the garden — In Hebrew, the phrase uses the singular "the tree of the garden" (עץ הגן), whereas the English translation "the trees of the garden" is plural. This grammatical surprise subtly shifts the meaning: Eve refers to a single tree (likely the Tree of Life or all trees collectively as one category), while the English version implies multiple trees, losing the precise referent.

esv.t: But of the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, God hath said, Ye shall not eat of it, neither shall ye touch it, lest ye die.

insights: vprE (ומפרי) — and from the fruit of — Hebrew grammar surprise: The word vprE uses a prefix M- that means 'from,' but the literal word order is 'and-from-fruit of the tree,' which English flattens into 'But of the fruit,' losing the grammatical construction that insists on partitive selection (some but not all of the fruit). In Hebrew, 'of' is not a separate preposition but is built directly into the word.

vayyomer hanakhash el ha'ishah lo mot t'mutun (ויאמר הנחש אל האשה לא מות תמתון) — Grammar surprise: The Hebrew uses an infinitive absolute (מות) before the finite verb (תמתון) to create an emphatic negation, 'you shall surely not die,' which is flattened to 'Ye shall not surely die' in English; this grammatical doubling is entirely invisible in translation, even though it completely reverses the emphasis from God's warning.

כי (ky) — for, because — The Hebrew word 'כי' is doing too much: it can mean 'for', 'because', 'that', 'surely', or 'when'. In verse 5, it serves as a hinge, introducing the serpent's twisted motivation, but also echoes 'כי' in Genesis 3:3, where Eve says 'because' God commanded not to eat — the same word frames both the command and its subversion, a nuance invisible in English's varied translations.

ותרא (vattar) — 'and she saw' — The verb 'to see' (ראה) appears only once in this verse in Hebrew, but the English translation 'saw' hides that the woman's act of 'seeing' (ותרא) is the very same root used in the earlier command not to 'eat' from the tree (Genesis 2:17) but never the word 'see'. The irony is that the first thing the woman does is 'see' (ראה) the tree's goodness, a verb absent from the prohibition, making her transgression hinge on an unmentioned sense.

ותפקחנה (vtpkhnh) — and were opened — The verb תִּפָּקַחְנָה shares the root פקח with an earlier key word: the serpent’s promise in Genesis 3:5 that 'your eyes will be opened' (וְנִפְקְחוּ עֵינֵיכֶם, wnpqḥw `ynykem). This root echo ties the fulfillment of the serpent’s prediction directly to the act of disobedience, showing that the 'opening' of eyes is not enlightenment but the painful awareness of nakedness and shame.

vayyitḥabba' hā'ādām (ויתחבא) — 'And hid himself' — The Hebrew verb ויתחבא is a reflexive hitpael form that implies Adam and Eve actively concealed themselves, yet Hebrew often omits the explicit subject with this verb, creating a sense of automatic or instinctive hiding that English must supply with 'hid themselves,' flattening the psychological nuance of a guilt-driven, almost involuntary concealment.

esv.t: And the Lord God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou?

insights: ykr' yhwh 'lhym 'l h'dm wy' mr lw 'ykh — And the Lord God called unto Adam, and said unto him, Where art thou? — The wordplay between "ykh" (where art thou) and the previous verse's "ykm" (your eyes) is lost: God asks “Where are you?” after the man and woman’s eyes were opened, linking their new awareness of nakedness to their physical hiding.

ויאמר (vyʼmr) את קלך (ʼt qlkh) שמעתי (shmʻty) בגן (bgn) ואירא (vʼyrʼ) כי (ky) עירם (ʻyrm) אנכי (ʼnky) ואחבא (vʼkhbʼ) — "And he said, I heard your voice in the garden, and I was afraid, because I was naked; and I hid myself." — Grammar surprise: The Hebrew word 'עירם' (ʻyrm, 'naked') is spelled without the letter א (aleph) that would normally appear in the feminine form 'ער

vayomer mi higid lecha ki eyrom atah (ויאמר מי הגיד לך כי עירם אתה) — 'And he said, Who told thee that thou wast naked?' — The root ג-י-ד (g-y-d) in 'higid' (הגיד, 'told') echoes the same root used earlier in Genesis 3:11's immediate context (the serpent 'told' Eve something, though not explicitly in the verse), but more strikingly it plays on the sound of the word 'eyrom' (עירם, 'naked'), creating a near-homophonic wordplay: the question 'Who told you?' literally sounds like 'Who made

וַיֹּאמֶר הָאָדָם הָאִשָּׁה אֲשֶׁר נָתַתָּה עִמָּדִי הִוא נָתְנָה לִּי מִן הָעֵץ וָאֹכֵל (ויאמר האדם האשה אשר נתתה עמדי הוא נתנה לי מן העץ ואכל) — 'And the man said, The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat.' — **Grammar surprise**: The Hebrew uses the feminine pronoun הִוא (hi) for "she," making grammatical agreement with "the

esv.t: And the Lord God said unto the woman, What is this that thou hast done? And the woman said, The serpent beguiled me, and I did eat.

insights: vayo'mer YHWH Elohim la-ishah mah zot asit vatomer ha-ishah ha-nachash hishi'ani vah'ochal (ויאמר יהוה אלהים לאשה מה זאת עשית ותאמר האשה הנחש השיאני ואכל) — The serpent beguiled me — The verb "hishi'ani" (השיאני) shares its root (ש-י-א) with the word for "falsehood" or "deception" (שוא), creating a root echo with Genesis 1:2 where "tohu va-vohu" (תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ) describes the formless

esv.t: And the Lord God said unto the serpent, Because thou hast done this, thou art cursed above all cattle, and above every beast of the field; upon thy belly shalt thou go, and dust shalt thou eat all the days of thy life:

insights: ארור (ARWR) — cursed — The root א-ר-ר appears here in the passive participle, but this very root was used earlier when God cursed the ground in Genesis 3:17 (ארורה האדמה, ARWRH HADMH). The same verbal root binds the serpent's curse to the earth's curse, a resonance lost in English which uses 'cursed' for both but does not signal the identical Hebrew root.

יְשׁוּפְךָ רֹאשׁ (yšwfk r’š) — 'it shall bruise your head' — The word 'yšwfk' (ישופך) contains the same three consonants (ש-ו-ף) as 'šwp' (שוף) found earlier in the verse for 'bruise,' creating a root echo that connects the serpent's seed bruising the heel with the bruising of the head, though in English 'bruise' translates both occurrences uniformly.

הרבה ארבה (harbah arbeh) — I will greatly multiply — The Hebrew uses the same root r-b-h twice in a row (infinitive absolute + imperfect), a grammatical construction that intensifies the verb, conveying 'multiplying I will multiply' rather than merely 'greatly multiply,' a doubling that English flattens into a single adverb.

לאדם (l'adam) — 'to Adam' — The Hebrew phrase 'to Adam' uses the same letters as the name 'Adamah' (ground/earth), creating a deliberate wordplay: the man formed from the earth is now cursed to struggle with it, as 'adam' and 'adamah' share the root אדם, which English cannot capture.

וצקש (והוציא) — 'and it shall bring forth' — The verb "תצמיח" (tatzmiach) shares a root (צ.מ.ח) with the earlier "תצמיח" in Genesis 2:9, where God causes every tree to grow from the ground. This root echo contrasts the curse: in chapter 2, God's immediate provision of fruitful trees; here, the ground now brings forth only thorns and thistles, mirroring the same act of causing growth but with a bitter reversal.

בזעת אפיך תאכל לחם — In the sweat of your face you will eat bread — A fascinating wordplay is lost in translation: the root עפר (dust/earth) occurs three times in this verse (עפר, האדמה from the root אדמ which is related to אדם/earthling, and then עפר again), creating an intentional echo that ties humanity's material origin (עפר/dust) to the ground (האדמה) from which Adam was taken, reinforcing the curse's core message: from dust you came, to dust you return.

esv.t: And Adam called his wife’s name Eve; because she was the mother of all living.

insights: vayiqra ha'adam shem ishto chawah ki hi haytah em kol chay — ויקרא האדם שם אשתו חוה כי הוא היתה אם כל חי — The grammar surprise is that the verb היתה (haytah, 'she was') is feminine singular, but the noun 'אם' (em, 'mother') is grammatically feminine as well, yet the English flattens the gender agreement that underscores Eve's role as the source of all living, a role that Hebrew explicitly frames in feminine verbal form.

esv.t: Unto Adam also and to his wife

insights: vayya‘as YHWH ’elohim le’adam ule’ishto kotnot ‘or vayyalbishem — root echo — The root עש״ה (do/make) appears as vayya‘as at the start of the verse and earlier in Genesis 3:21 as the same verb used for God making the garments, but also echoes the same root used in Genesis 1 for God's acts of creation, linking the divine making of clothing to the original making of the world.

ויאמר יהוה אלהים — And the Lord God said — The root אמר (to say) appears here and earlier in Genesis 1:3 (ויאמר אלהים), creating a deliberate echo between God's first creative speech and this judgment speech, highlighting the contrast between the original good creation and the consequence of disobedience.

esv.t: Therefore the Lord God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken.

insights: vayeshalkehu (וישלחהו) — sent him forth — The Hebrew verb וישלחהו contains three layers: 'he sent,' 'him,' and a directive suffix, packing a full English clause 'he sent him forth' into one word, a grammatical density invisible in translation.

esv.t: So he drove out the man; and he placed at the east of the garden of Eden Cherubims, and a flaming sword which turned every way, to keep the way of the tree of life.

insights: וישכן (vyškn) — 'and he placed' — The verb וישכן shares a root (שכן) with the word 'Mishkan' (Tabernacle) and earlier in Genesis appears in the story of Cain building a city (Gen 4:17, ויהי בונה עיר), creating a subtle echo of divine dwelling and human settlement that is lost in English translation.
